---
title: "DICOM as a Data Model"
lang: en-GB
---

## The DICOM Standard {#sec-dicom-norma}

### The problem before DICOM

Until the early 1980s, digital medical imaging was produced by equipment
whose storage format was defined exclusively by each manufacturer. A
General Electric computed tomography scanner could not export images to
a Siemens workstation; a Philips magnetic resonance system stored its
files in a format incompatible with any other system. This fragmentation
created an absolute dependency on the vendor — the so-called
*vendor lock-in* — and made it impossible to share images between
institutions, even when such sharing would have been clinically relevant
[@Pianykh2012].

The practical consequences were significant: radiological films
circulated physically in cardboard envelopes, with risk of loss and
deterioration; comparison with previous examinations depended on the
availability of the physical archive; and integration with hospital
information systems was non-existent. A paradox thus arose: acquisition
equipment was technologically advanced, yet the information it produced
remained in a proprietary, isolated format [@Huang2010].

### Definition, scope, and governance

The DICOM standard (*Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine*)
is the international standard for digital medical imaging.
Published as ISO 12052:2017 and managed by MITA (*Medical
Imaging & Technology Alliance*), a division of NEMA (*National Electrical
Manufacturers Association*), the standard is continuously updated —
there are no fixed major versions, but dated editions; the current
edition is designated PS3 2025e [@NEMA_DICOM2025] (as of April 2026).

The standard is organised into 22 active parts, each covering a specific
domain: from introduction and conformance definition (PS3.1–PS3.2) to
data structure (PS3.5–PS3.6), message services (PS3.7), network protocol
(PS3.8), file format (PS3.10), and security profiles (PS3.15). More than
thirty working groups contribute to its ongoing maintenance and evolution.

In terms of scope, the standard covers all relevant clinical imaging
modalities: computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance (MR),
ultrasonography (US), digital radiography (DX), nuclear medicine (NM),
radiotherapy, ophthalmology, and others. The standard's coverage is
therefore transversal to all digital medical imaging
[@NEMA_DICOM2025; @ISO12052].

### Functional capabilities and ecosystem

The DICOM standard covers the complete lifecycle of medical imaging: from
**producing** — creating images in a standardised format on any equipment;
**storing** — preserving images with all associated metadata;
**transmitting** — sending between devices and institutions;
**querying** — finding studies by patient, date, or modality;
**displaying** — presenting images on diagnostic workstations with
appropriate parameters; to **printing** — sending to film or paper
printers [@NEMA_DICOM2025].

From an ecosystem perspective, the standard benefits all stakeholders:
**clinicians** gain rapid access to images and reports, including via
remote diagnosis; **patients** benefit from faster care and data that
can be transferred between institutions; **hospitals** can purchase
equipment from different manufacturers with guaranteed compatibility;
and the **industry** has access to a broad, competitive market, provided
its products comply with the standard [@Pianykh2012].

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: Without DICOM (proprietary formats, isolated islands)
           vs With DICOM (central node, universal interoperability).
  Teal palette: #0d7c8c main, #1ab3a6 accent, #0a3d4a dark.
  Width: 700px, height: 220px.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin: 1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 700 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:700px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <!-- Background -->
  <rect width="700" height="220" rx="12" fill="#f8fbfc"/>
  <!-- WITHOUT DICOM column -->
  <text x="170" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#b91c1c">Without DICOM</text>
  <!-- Manufacturer icons — islands -->
  <rect x="30"  y="45" width="90" height="36" rx="6" fill="#fca5a5"/>
  <text x="75"  y="68" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#7f1d1d">GE (CT)</text>
  <rect x="140" y="45" width="90" height="36" rx="6" fill="#fca5a5"/>
  <text x="185" y="68" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#7f1d1d">Siemens (MR)</text>
  <rect x="30"  y="105" width="90" height="36" rx="6" fill="#fca5a5"/>
  <text x="75"  y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#7f1d1d">Philips (US)</text>
  <rect x="140" y="105" width="90" height="36" rx="6" fill="#fca5a5"/>
  <text x="185" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#7f1d1d">Canon (DX)</text>
  <!-- X connectors -->
  <line x1="120" y1="63" x2="140" y2="63" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4"/>
  <line x1="120" y1="123" x2="140" y2="123" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4"/>
  <line x1="75" y1="81" x2="75" y2="105" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4"/>
  <line x1="185" y1="81" x2="185" y2="105" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4"/>
  <text x="152" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" fill="#b91c1c">✗</text>
  <!-- Separator -->
  <line x1="350" y1="20" x2="350" y2="200" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="6"/>
  <!-- WITH DICOM column -->
  <text x="530" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#0d7c8c">With DICOM</text>
  <!-- Central DICOM node -->
  <ellipse cx="530" cy="110" rx="52" ry="30" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="530" y="115" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">DICOM</text>
  <!-- Satellites -->
  <rect x="415" y="45" width="70" height="28" rx="5" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="450" y="64" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff">GE (CT)</text>
  <rect x="415" y="140" width="70" height="28" rx="5" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="450" y="159" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff">Philips (US)</text>
  <rect x="600" y="45" width="70" height="28" rx="5" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="635" y="64" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff">Siemens (MR)</text>
  <rect x="600" y="140" width="70" height="28" rx="5" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="635" y="159" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#fff">Canon (DX)</text>
  <!-- Connection lines -->
  <line x1="485" y1="94" x2="483" y2="73" stroke="#0a3d4a" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="485" y1="126" x2="483" y2="144" stroke="#0a3d4a" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="575" y1="94" x2="600" y2="73" stroke="#0a3d4a" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="575" y1="126" x2="600" y2="144" stroke="#0a3d4a" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- Caption -->
  <text x="350" y="208" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 4.1 — Proprietary fragmentation / vendor lock-in (left) vs. DICOM interoperability (right)
  </text>
</svg>
</div>
```

## The DICOM Data Model {#sec-dicom-modelo}

### From the real world to the DICOM world

The conceptual starting point of the DICOM standard is the mapping
between entities in the real world (in a clinical context) and their
formal, standardised representation in computer systems. In the real
world, a patient has a name, identifier, date of birth, sex, and weight;
in the standard, each of these attributes is encoded through a
standardised numeric code (TAG), a well-defined data type, and a value
that follows precise formatting rules [@DICOM_PS36].

This mapping is ensured by a **dictionary** and guarantees that,
regardless of the equipment manufacturer, storage system, or
visualisation workstation, the same attribute is always represented in
the same way. The concept "patient name" is always encoded by the same
dictionary entry, in the same format; "date of birth" always follows the
`YYYYMMDD` notation; "sex" accepts only the enumerated values `M`, `F`,
or `O`. Uniformity is not a recommendation — it is an obligation imposed
by the standard.

| Clinical attribute      | DICOM TAG    | Example value   |
|-------------------------|--------------|-----------------|
| Patient's name          | (0010,0010)  | `Andrade^Maria` |
| Identifier              | (0010,0020)  | `1234567`       |
| Date of birth           | (0010,0030)  | `19650412`      |
| Weight (kg)             | (0010,1030)  | `57.3`          |
| Sex                     | (0010,0040)  | `F`             |

: Example mapping from the real world to the DICOM world [@DICOM_PS36]. {#tbl-mapeamento}

### Data Elements, TAGs, and the Dictionary (PS3.6)

The fundamental unit of information in the DICOM standard is the **Data
Element**. In the file — the so-called *wire format* (PS3.5) — each
Data Element is encoded with exactly four fields: the TAG, the VR
(*Value Representation*), the *Value Length*, and the *Value Field*
[@DICOM_PS35]. These four fields are everything that exists in the
byte-stream transmitted or stored; there are no other fields in the file.

The **TAG** is a pair of 16-bit hexadecimal integers written in the form
`(gggg,eeee)`, where `gggg` is the group number and `eeee` the element
number. The group number thematically groups attributes: group `0010`
contains all patient attributes, group `0008` the modality identification
attributes, group `0020` the study attributes, group `0028` the image
parameters, and group `7FE0` contains the pixel data itself
[@DICOM_PS36].

The **Data Dictionary**, defined in part PS3.6 of the standard,
constitutes the official registry of all normalised Data Elements. Each
dictionary entry is a six-field tuple — but note: this is a different
level from the Data Element in the file. Of the six fields, only three
(TAG, Name, and VR) are encoded in the *wire format* (PS3.5); the
remaining three (VM, Type, and Description) belong exclusively to the
dictionary model — they define the semantic contract of the attribute
but do not appear as fields in the file [@DICOM_PS35; @DICOM_PS36].
The following figure illustrates this distinction:

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: two levels of the DICOM Data Element.
  Upper level (file): 4 fields encoded in the wire format.
  Lower level (dictionary): 6 fields of the PS3.6 entry.
  Arrows indicating which of the 6 dictionary fields correspond
  to the file fields (TAG, VR) and which are model-only
  (VM, Type, Description). Name has no direct field in the wire format.
  Teal palette.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 700 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:700px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="700" height="260" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>

  <!-- LABEL Data Element level -->
  <text x="14" y="28" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">Data Element — serialised fields (PS3.5, §7.1)</text>

  <!-- 4 wire format blocks -->
  <rect x="14"  y="34" width="110" height="44" rx="6" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="69"  y="53" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">TAG</text>
  <text x="69"  y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"  fill="#7dd3d0">on wire</text>

  <rect x="136" y="34" width="110" height="44" rx="6" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="191" y="53" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">VR</text>
  <text x="191" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"  fill="#b2ebf2">on wire</text>

  <rect x="258" y="34" width="140" height="44" rx="6" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="328" y="53" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Value Length</text>
  <text x="328" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"  fill="#e0f7f5">on wire</text>

  <rect x="410" y="34" width="270" height="44" rx="6" fill="#e2f4f3" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="545" y="53" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">Value Field</text>
  <text x="545" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"  fill="#0d7c8c">on wire</text>

  <!-- Separator -->
  <line x1="14" y1="106" x2="686" y2="106" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="5"/>

  <!-- LABEL dictionary level -->
  <text x="14" y="122" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#6b7280">Data Dictionary entry (PS3.6)</text>

  <!-- 6 dictionary blocks -->
  <!-- TAG — corresponds to file -->
  <rect x="14"  y="130" width="95" height="44" rx="6" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="61"  y="149" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">TAG</text>
  <text x="61"  y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"  fill="#7dd3d0">(group, elem.)</text>

  <!-- Name — dictionary only -->
  <rect x="120" y="130" width="95" height="44" rx="6" fill="#e5e7eb" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="167" y="149" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#374151">Name</text>
  <text x="167" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"  fill="#6b7280">dictionary only</text>

  <!-- VR — corresponds to file -->
  <rect x="226" y="130" width="95" height="44" rx="6" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="273" y="149" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">VR</text>
  <text x="273" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"  fill="#b2ebf2">data type</text>

  <!-- VM — dictionary only -->
  <rect x="332" y="130" width="95" height="44" rx="6" fill="#e5e7eb" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="379" y="149" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#374151">VM</text>
  <text x="379" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"  fill="#6b7280">dictionary only</text>

  <!-- Type — dictionary only -->
  <rect x="438" y="130" width="95" height="44" rx="6" fill="#e5e7eb" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="485" y="149" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#374151">Type</text>
  <text x="485" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"  fill="#6b7280">dictionary only</text>

  <!-- Description — dictionary only -->
  <rect x="544" y="130" width="140" height="44" rx="6" fill="#e5e7eb" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="614" y="149" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#374151">Description</text>
  <text x="614" y="165" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"  fill="#6b7280">dictionary only</text>

  <!-- Correspondence arrows: TAG (file) ↔ TAG (dictionary) -->
  <line x1="69" y1="78" x2="61" y2="130" stroke="#0a3d4a" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4"/>
  <!-- Correspondence arrows: VR (file) ↔ VR (dictionary) -->
  <line x1="191" y1="78" x2="273" y2="130" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4"/>

  <!-- Legend -->
  <rect x="14"  y="196" width="14" height="10" rx="2" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="32"  y="206" font-size="9" fill="#374151">Field serialised in the Data Element (wire format)</text>
  <rect x="220" y="196" width="14" height="10" rx="2" fill="#e5e7eb" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="238" y="206" font-size="9" fill="#374151">Field in dictionary only (semantic contract, not serialised)</text>

  <text x="350" y="250" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 4.3 — Data Element in the file (PS3.5) vs. Data Dictionary entry (PS3.6)
  </text>
</svg>
</div>
```


The **Type** field indicates the requirement level: `1` = mandatory and
non-empty; `1C` = conditional; `2` = mandatory but may be empty; `3` =
optional. This distinction is relevant for validating the conformance of
DICOM systems.

The **VM** (*Value Multiplicity*) field indicates how many values are
permitted in the *Value Field* of a given attribute. VM `1` means a
single value; VM `1-n` means one or more values; VM `2-2n` means an
even number of values. For example, the attribute
`(0008,0061) Modalities in Study` has VM `1-n` — it may contain a
single modality (`CT`) or several (`CT`, `MR`, `US`), depending on the
number of series of different types present in the study. Note that VM
is a restriction declared in the dictionary; the *Value Field* in the
file does not explicitly carry the number of values — the receiver infers
it from the length (*Value Length*) and the type (VR)
[@DICOM_PS35; @DICOM_PS36].


### Value Representations (VR)

*Value Representations* (VR) define the data type of each Data Element.
The standard specifies 27 VR types, constituting the DICOM equivalent
of data types in a programming language — `int`, `string`, `date`, among
others [@DICOM_PS35].

Each VR is identified by a two-character ASCII code. The most relevant
VRs in clinical and technical practice are as follows:

| VR   | Name              | Description                                     | Example              |
|------|-------------------|-------------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| `PN` | Person Name       | Name in `Family^Given^Middle` format            | `Andrade^Maria`      |
| `DA` | Date              | Date in `YYYYMMDD` format                       | `19650412`           |
| `DS` | Decimal String    | Decimal number represented as text              | `57.3`               |
| `CS` | Code String       | Controlled-domain code                          | `CT`, `MR`, `US`     |
| `UI` | Unique Identifier | Globally unique UID (dot-separated syntax)      | `1.2.840.10008...`   |
| `US` | Unsigned Short    | 16-bit unsigned integer                         | `512`                |
| `OW` | Other Word        | Binary data (e.g. pixels)                       | *(binary data)*      |
| `LO` | Long String       | Text up to 64 characters                        | `Hospital UTAD`      |

: Main Value Representations of the DICOM standard [@DICOM_PS35]. {#tbl-vr}

Note that the VR `OW` (*Other Word*) is used for the field
`(7FE0,0010) Pixel Data`, which contains the binary image data — which
may be in raw or compressed format (JPEG, JPEG 2000, RLE). The choice
of compression format is negotiated between devices during the
establishment of the DICOM association.

### Integrated view: TAG + VR + VM

The three concepts — TAG, VR, and VM — articulate in a precise
relationship: the TAG identifies **what**, the VR defines **how** the
value is encoded, and the VM indicates **how many** values are permitted
in the *Value Field*. This triple contract is declared in the Data
Dictionary for each normalised attribute [@DICOM_PS36].

The following table illustrates the integrated view of a set of
representative Data Elements:

| TAG          | Name                    | VR   | VM  | Notes                              |
|--------------|-------------------------|------|-----|------------------------------------|
| `(0008,0060)`| Modality                | `CS` | 1   | Values: `CT`, `MR`, `US`, `DX`…   |
| `(0010,0010)`| Patient's Name          | `PN` | 1   | Format: `Family^Given`             |
| `(0010,0030)`| Patient's Birth Date    | `DA` | 1   | Format: `YYYYMMDD`                 |
| `(0010,1030)`| Patient's Weight        | `DS` | 1   | Weight in kg as decimal string     |
| `(0020,000D)`| Study Instance UID      | `UI` | 1   | Globally unique study UID          |
| `(0028,0010)`| Rows                    | `US` | 1   | Number of rows; 16-bit integer     |
| `(7FE0,0010)`| Pixel Data              | `OW` | 1   | Binary image data                  |

: Integrated view of selected Data Elements [@DICOM_PS35;@DICOM_PS36]. {#tbl-de-integrado}

A complete Data Element with a real value looks as follows:

```
TAG         VR    Value Length   Value Field
(0010,0010) PN    16             COUTO^FERNANDO
```

In this example, the TAG `(0010,0010)` identifies the attribute "patient
name"; the VR `PN` specifies that the value follows the *Person Name*
format; the *Value Length* indicates that the value occupies 16 bytes;
and the *Value Field* contains the name encoded in the `Family^Given`
convention [@DICOM_PS35].

## Entities, IODs, and Modules {#sec-dicom-iod}

### The Patient → Study → Series → Image hierarchy

The DICOM information model organises data in a four-level hierarchy
that reflects the real clinical workflow. Each level corresponds to an
**Information Entity** (IE) and contains one or more elements of the
immediately lower level [@DICOM_PS33].

**Patient** — represents the person. Stores demographic data: name,
identifier, date of birth, sex. A patient may have multiple studies
over time.

**Study** — corresponds to a clinical imaging episode: a date, a
description, a referring physician, and a globally unique study UID.
Each study contains one or more series.

**Series** — corresponds to a specific acquisition within the study.
It identifies the modality (`CT`, `MR`…), the series number, and the
patient's position. A brain CT study may have, for example, five series:
non-contrast, contrast-enhanced, bone reconstruction, 3D reconstruction,
and localiser image (*scout*).

**Image** — is the atomic unit of information: an individual image, with
its pixel dimensions, bit depth, compression parameters, and above all,
the *Pixel Data* itself. Each image is identified by a globally unique,
non-repeating UID generated by the acquisition equipment at capture time.

The practical consequence of this hierarchy is direct: a single
multi-series brain CT can generate approximately 1,500 distinct DICOM
files — each containing all the metadata from the four levels (Patient,
Study, Series, Image) plus the pixels of that specific image [@Huang2010].

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: Patient → Study → Series → Image hierarchy.
  Four cascading boxes, indented, with containment arrows.
  Teal palette.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 640 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:640px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="640" height="200" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>
  <!-- Patient -->
  <rect x="20"  y="20" width="580" height="38" rx="7" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="40"  y="44" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Patient</text>
  <text x="160" y="44" font-size="11" fill="#7dd3d0">name · ID · date of birth · sex</text>
  <!-- Study -->
  <rect x="50"  y="68" width="520" height="34" rx="7" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="70"  y="90" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Study</text>
  <text x="160" y="90" font-size="11" fill="#b2ebf2">date · description · referring physician · Study UID</text>
  <!-- Series -->
  <rect x="80"  y="112" width="460" height="34" rx="7" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="100" y="134" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Series</text>
  <text x="185" y="134" font-size="11" fill="#e0f7f5">modality · series no. · patient position</text>
  <!-- Image -->
  <rect x="110" y="156" width="400" height="30" rx="7" fill="#e2f4f3" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="130" y="176" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">Image</text>
  <text x="210" y="176" font-size="11" fill="#0d7c8c">pixels · dimensions · bits · unique UID</text>
  <!-- Containment arrows -->
  <line x1="38" y1="58" x2="38" y2="68" stroke="#7dd3d0" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <line x1="68" y1="102" x2="68" y2="112" stroke="#b2ebf2" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <line x1="98" y1="146" x2="98" y2="156" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="3" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#94a3b8"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <!-- Caption -->
  <text x="320" y="197" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 4.3 — DICOM information entity hierarchy (PS3.3, §A.1)
  </text>
</svg>
</div>
```

### Information Object Definitions (IOD)

An **IOD** (*Information Object Definition*) is the formal specification
of the set of attributes that describes a particular type of DICOM
object. It is not a file — it is a normative definition: it states which
attributes are mandatory, conditional, and optional for a given type of
medical image or document [@DICOM_PS33].

The standard defines a composite IOD (*Composite IOD*) for each relevant
modality: CT Image IOD, MR Image IOD, CR Image IOD, US Image IOD, and
others. A fundamental point that deserves explicit attention: **a
composite IOD always describes a single image** — not a study or a
series. The IOD is instantiated once per image; a CT with 300 slices
generates 300 instances of the CT Image IOD, each in an independent
DICOM file.

Each instance carries with it the attributes of *all* hierarchical
levels: patient, study, series, and image data. The logical grouping
into series and studies is not performed by physical references between
files — it is inferred from shared UIDs: all images in the same series
share the same `Series Instance UID`; all images in the same study share
the same `Study Instance UID`. The hierarchy exists in the metadata of
each individual file; any system reading them can reconstruct it without
requiring external structures [@DICOM_PS33].

Each composite IOD is derived from a general entity model (*DICOM
Composite Instance IOD Information Model*) that formalises the
relationships between Patient, Study, Equipment, Frame of Reference,
Series, and the various types of possible instances (Image, SR Document,
Waveform, Surface, etc.) [@DICOM_PS33].

For example, the **CR Image IOD** (*Computed Radiography*) includes,
in terms of information entities (IE): Patient, Study, Series, Equipment,
and Image — all present in each individual file. Each IE is specified by
the modules that belong to it, with their respective usage level.

### Information modules and usage types (M/C/U)

Each IE of an IOD is composed of **modules** — thematic groupings of
related Data Elements. A module always has an associated usage level
[@DICOM_PS33]:

- **M** (*Mandatory*) — mandatory; must be present and populated.
- **C** (*Conditional*) — conditional; mandatory only when a specific
  condition is met (e.g., the *Contrast/Bolus* module is `C` —
  mandatory if contrast medium was used).
- **U** (*User Optional*) — optional; may be included or omitted by
  the manufacturer.

The **Patient Module** (reference C.7.1.1), mandatory (`M`) in all
composite IODs, groups the patient identification attributes:
`(0010,0010) Patient's Name` (Type 2), `(0010,0020) Patient ID`
(Type 2), `(0010,0030) Patient's Birth Date` (Type 3), and
`(0010,0040) Patient's Sex` (Type 2), among others. Note that Type 2
means the attribute must be present but may have an empty value —
something clinically relevant in emergency contexts [@DICOM_PS33].

### The DICOM file: storage format

The composite IOD defines *which* attributes describe an image; part
PS3.10 of the standard defines *how* those attributes are physically
stored in a file. These are two distinct planes: the model and the
packaging.

A DICOM file begins with a **preamble** of 128 bytes (reserved, usually
null) followed by the ASCII prefix `DICM`. Next comes the **File Meta
Information** — a set of group `0002` Data Elements describing the file
itself: the format version, the stored SOP Class, the instance UID, and
the negotiated Transfer Syntax (which determines the compression format
of the Pixel Data). After this header follows the sequence of IOD Data
Elements, ordered by ascending TAG [@DICOM_PS310].

The Transfer Syntax is an important detail: it tells the receiver
whether the Pixel Data is in raw format (uncompressed), JPEG Baseline,
JPEG 2000, or RLE. This information is in the file header — not in the
Pixel Data itself — which allows the receiver to select the correct
decoder before reading the pixels.

A representative excerpt from a CT DICOM file, as displayed in a
metadata viewer such as `dcmdump` (DCMTK), illustrates the sequence of
Data Elements ordered by TAG:

```
(0008,0060) CS  Modality              [CT]
(0008,1030) LO  Study Description     [BRAIN CT]
(0010,0010) PN  Patient's Name        [COUTO^FERNANDO]
(0010,0020) LO  Patient ID            [987654]
(0010,0030) DA  Patient's Birth Date  [19650412]
(0028,0010) US  Rows                  [512]
(0028,0011) US  Columns               [512]
(7FE0,0010) OW  Pixel Data            [binary — 524288 bytes]
```

Note that all metadata from the four hierarchical levels (Patient,
Study, Series, Image) are present in each individual DICOM file. This
intentional redundancy ensures that any file is self-sufficient and can
be interpreted without depending on an external database
[@DICOM_PS310; @Pianykh2012].

## Summary and Exercises {#sec-dicom-modelo-sintese}

### Consolidation exercises {.unnumbered}

1. A CT DICOM file contains the Data Element
   `(0010,0030) DA 8 19820915`. Interpret each field (TAG, VR,
   *Value Length*, *Value Field*), indicating the thematic group to
   which the TAG belongs and the clinical meaning of the value.

2. Explain why a composite CT Image IOD with 300 images generates 300
   independent DICOM files rather than a single file. How is the
   Study/Series hierarchy reconstructed from those files?

3. Distinguish the fields that physically exist in a serialised Data
   Element from the fields that belong exclusively to the corresponding
   entry in the Data Dictionary. Why is this distinction relevant for
   implementing a DICOM system?

4. A Data Dictionary entry has VM `1-n` and Type `2`. What do these
   two values mean together for a system validating the conformance
   of a received DICOM file?

5. The 128-byte preamble of a DICOM file is corrupted. Discuss the
   impact of this corruption on the ability of a PS3.10-conformant
   system to process the file.
